coredata.dc:title: Green carbon dots synthesized from Chlorella Sorokiniana microalgae biochar for chrome detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Pena, Aline C.C.
Raymundo, Lucas Manique
Trierweiler, Luciane F.
Gutterres, Mariliz
coredata.dc:description: In the quest to find an economical and accurate method of detecting toxic metals as well as finding an application for biochar as a fuel, this work used microalgae biochar to sinter Carbon Dots (CDs) that can be used as pathways for metallic ions detection. Carbon Dots from microalgae biochar (generated in pyrolysis) were synthesized through thermochemical depolymerization with KMnO4 at different concentrations. After this step, the purification of the nanoparticles was performed. The characterization of CDs was performed through fluorescence intensity, Zeta Potential, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The results showed that all samples exhibited fluorescence, samples synthesized with KMnO4 at concentrations of 8.54 % and 10.0 % (CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07, respectively) showed the highest fluorescence intensity and good stability. When adding Cr (VI) and Cr (III) to the CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07 samples, it is possible to notice that there was fluorescence quenching as the metal ion concentration increased, except for the CD-MBK07 sample for Cr (III), at a length of 350 nm, indicating that this sample shows selectivity for extinction only for Cr (VI).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microalgae
Biochar
Fluorescence
Chrome

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of microalgae biomass and biochar
Fluorescent detection of Cr (VI) and Cr (III)
Characterization of microalgae biomass and biochar
Detection of Cr (VI) and Cr (III) by fluorescence Spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the carbon dots synthesis step.
Thermogravimetric analysis of the dry biomass of microalgae Chlorella Sorokiniana (a) and biochar generated from microalgae biomass (b).
FTIR of dry biomass and biochar from microalgae.

Biochar fluorescence emission spectrum with different concentrations of KMnO4 A) CD-MB B) CD-MBK02 C) CD-MBK03 D) CD-MBK04 E) CD-MBK06 F) CD-MBK07 and G) statistical analysis of fluorescence intensity.
FTIR of samples CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07.
AFM of samples CD-MBK06 (a) and CD-MBK07 (b).
Fluorescence spectrum at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm with growing concentrations of Cr (VI): CD-MBK06 (A) and CD-MBK07 (B) and Cr (III): CD-MBK06 (C) and CD-MBK07 (D).
Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) and the concentration of Cr (VI) in water for CD-MBK06 (A) and CD-MBK07 (B) and Cr (III) for CD-MBK06 (C) and CD-MBK07 (D).
Fluorescence quenching efficiencies of CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07 after addition of Cr (VI) and Cr (III).
Fluorescence quenching at 350 nm excitation wavelength with different concentrations of Cr (VI) with CD-MBK07 (A) and Cr (III) with CD-MBK07 (B).
Central composite design to evaluate chemical compound and concentration.
The coefficient of determination and linear relationship between the Cr (VI) or Cr (III) concentration and the FI at 350 nm excitation wavelength.

head.ce:title: Green carbon dots synthesized from Chlorella Sorokiniana microalgae biochar for chrome detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory for Leather and Environmental Studies (LACOURO), Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory for Leather and Environmental Studies (LACOURO)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory for Leather and Environmental Studies (LACOURO), Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Group of Intensification, Modeling, Simulation, Control, and Optimization of Process (GIMSCOP), Chemical Engineering Department, Federal University of Rio Grande do Sul (UFRGS), Av. Luiz Englert s/n, Porto Alegre, RS 90040-040, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Group of Intensification, Modeling, Simulation, Control, and Optimization of Process (GIMSCOP)
Federal University of Rio Grande do Sul (UFRGS)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Group of Intensification, Modeling, Simulation, Control, and Optimization of Process (GIMSCOP), Chemical Engineering Department, Federal University of Rio Grande do Sul (UFRGS), Av. Luiz Englert s/n, Porto Alegre, RS 90040-040, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the quest to find an economical and accurate method of detecting toxic metals as well as finding an application for biochar as a fuel, this work used microalgae biochar to sinter Carbon Dots (CDs) that can be used as pathways for metallic ions detection. Carbon Dots from microalgae biochar (generated in pyrolysis) were synthesized through thermochemical depolymerization with KMnO4 at different concentrations. After this step, the purification of the nanoparticles was performed. The characterization of CDs was performed through fluorescence intensity, Zeta Potential, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The results showed that all samples exhibited fluorescence, samples synthesized with KMnO4 at concentrations of 8.54 % and 10.0 % (CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07, respectively) showed the highest fluorescence intensity and good stability. When adding Cr (VI) and Cr (III) to the CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07 samples, it is possible to notice that there was fluorescence quenching as the metal ion concentration increased, except for the CD-MBK07 sample for Cr (III), at a length of 350 nm, indicating that this sample shows selectivity for extinction only for Cr (VI).

ce:section.ce:para: Microalgae have been extensively studied for wastewater phytoremediation. Phytoremediation by microalgae is reported in the literature on wastewater: municipal [1,55] A, leather industry [40,41], pharmaceutical industry [6,34], dairy industry [7,13], swine [29,31] and others. The advantage of removing pollutants from wastewater with microalgae is that it is eco-friendly and economical. Furthermore, biomass production during the phytoremediation process makes it possible to transform it into new value-added products. Due to the high lipid values found in the biomass, many studies report the extraction of bio-oil that can be used for several purposes, including biofuels. The pyrolysis process, one of the main methods used to transform biomass into bio-oil, a liquid bio-fuel intermediate, also generates co-products such as biochar and pyrolysis gas. Microalgae are important in pyrolysis biorefineries, because of the liquid products generated, which have yields and fuel qualities superior to those obtained with other biomasses. Biochar is generated in high yields (15–43 % in weight from biomasses), and biochar production should increase as pyrolysis-based biorefineries are created and developed [26,57]. Consequently, it is essential to find new applications and develop new products from biochar generated in the pyrolysis of biomass, such as microalgae.

Studies have been reported that use biochar as additives for carbon sequestration [50], agricultural soils [21], and adsorbents [32]. Recently, [43] proposed the transformation into carbon nanoparticles and applied as sensors for toxic metal ions (Pb2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, and Ni2+) in an aqueous medium. Using microalgae biochar for the synthesis of CDs has many advantages such as availability, low toxicity, and the possibility of combining carbohydrates, lipids, and proteins to form the carbon dots structure. Besides these advantages, carbon dots (CDs) are nanoparticles with relevant electronic and optical properties that have gained attention in recent years due to numerous application areas, such as catalysis [22,28], diagnosis, and drug sensing [2,33], bioimaging [30,66] and metal sensors [11,48].

Unique characteristics, such as excellent fluorescence, photostability, high biocompatibility, high water solubility, low synthesis cost, and their structures with bonded groups on the surface of carbons (which provide sites to interact with ions and chemicals) make these CDs nanomaterials excellent biosensors [14,17]. Interactions between CDs and analytes can produce electron transfer and change the energy of the fluorescence resonance [58]. This process changes the fluorescence intensity, allowing CDs to act as sensors emitting measurable response signals [35,62].

Currently, there is significant research on CDs as sensors for detecting metal ions. The chromium metal, in its various forms, can be present in several industrial processes, such as ink manufacture [53], textile industry [37], metal coatings [46], and industrial leather tanning [3]. Although chromium in its trivalent form (Cr(III)) is recognized as a non-toxic and stable metal, it can be oxidized to hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) [15]. Cr (VI) is carcinogenic and, even in small amounts (according to the National Environmental Council of Brazil, the allowed limit for Cr(VI) for treated wastewater discharge is 0.1 mg.L−1 and 1.0 mg.L−1 for Cr(III) [9], poses high risks to living beings [60], requiring strict control so that it is not released into the environment. Several detection techniques for the control of chromium have already been reached in the literature, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [25], high-performance liquid chromatography [52], ion chromatography [63], colorimetry [49] electrochemical technique [27], and spectrophotometry [36].

In recent years, the fluorescence methodology has stood out for its simplicity, convenience, and high sensitivity in metal detection [48]. [20] used Poria cocos to synthesize carbon quantum dots from the hydrothermal method. These nanoparticles showed good fluorescence stability, and when in contact with Cr (VI), they formed a non-fluorescent complex (fluorescence quenching). They presented a good linear relationship with Cr (VI) concentration in 1–100 μM with a detection limit of 0.25 μM. Other materials were used for the synthesis of carbon dots for application as chromium sensors, such as the substrate of kyanite (Bardhan et al., 2022), wine lees [59], Carica papaya [47], lemon peel [61], and bacterium [23].

To the best of our knowledge, there is a single research work where microalgae biochar was used for carbon dot synthesis, but it did not evaluate CDs as chromium metal sensors [43]. Thus, this work presented the green synthesis of carbon dots from microalgae biochar through a hydrothermal method, the characterization of CDs, and the evaluation of CDs as sensors of Cr (III) and Cr (VI) via quenching fluorescence.

The dry biomass (DB) from the microalgae Chlorella Sorokiniana was donated from Olson Nutrition LTDA. Analytical grade chemicals were used as received from local suppliers: potassiumpermanganate(KMnO4),and acetone. Ultra-pure water was used throughout this study (Gehaka).

The dry biomass (DB) of the microalgae Chlorella Sorokiniana was used to prepare the biochar. The pyrolysis reaction was carried out on a laboratory scale system developed by group of Intensification, Modeling, Simulation, Control, and Optimization of Process (GIMSCOP). The reactor information is presented in the supplementary material.

The reactor was loaded with 150 g of dry microalgae biomass, the system was heated from room temperature to a final temperature of 500 °C, with a heating rate of approximately 5 °C.min−1. During heating, liquid products were collected and microalgae biochar (MB) was generated and remained inside the reactor. After reaching the final pyrolysis temperature, the heating system was turned off, and the reactor cooled to room temperature. The MB was crushed and sieved with a 1 mm mesh.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of microalgae biomass and biochar
ce:section.ce:para: DB and MB were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). FT-IR spectra were collected using a Frontier FT-IR spectrophotometer with a sampler (PerkinElmer) from 4000 a 650 cm−1 with 16 scan speeds, and spectral analyses were performed using Spectragryph software version 1.1 (Spectroscopy Ninja). TGA was carried out on SDT Q600 V20.9 Build 20 (Perkin Elmer). The sample was analyzed in the temperature range from 28 °C to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C.min−1 under the flow of N2 atmosphere.

The synthesis of carbon dots was performed from MB. The depolymerization of MB was evaluated using one variable (concentration) in a 1 × 5 central compound experimental design. There was one level for the choice of chemical compound: potassium permanganate (KMnO4), called MBK. Preliminary tests were performed with H2SO4, however, these samples showed low fluorescence intensity, and therefore, were discarded and the study followed using only KMnO4. The chemical concentrations evaluated were 0, 1.46, 5, 8.54, and 10 %, as shown in Table 1 .

All samples were added to Erlenmeyer flasks, and 5 % MB was added with water (20 mL). The samples were autoclaved for 3 hours at 15 psi and 120 °C. After depolymerization, the samples were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min at room temperature to separate the larger particles. The acetone (20 mL) was added to the liquid phase forming a second phase. The phases were separated by centrifugation (4000 rpm for 20 min) [19]. The upper phase was rotating evaporation until dry. The solids were obtained after two more extractions with acetone.

Afterward, solids were resuspended in water and ultrasonicated for 20 min (50 % of 200 W) (Branson, W450®). The samples were purified with a dialysis bag (Mw = 12,000–14,000 Da) for 24 h and filtered with 0.2 μm cellulose acetate membrane (Merck Millipore) to remove impurities. The yield calculation for each sample was performed by calculating the amount of sample produced mg per 5 g of biochar. The Carbon Dots by microalgae biochar (CD-MBK) were dried and resuspended in water at 0.1 mg.mL −1 and maintained at 4 °C until the analyses were performed. A schematic of the steps for the synthesis of carbon dots are shown in Fig. 1 .

Absorption spectra were recorded on a spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu). Measurements were carried out in the region of 400 to 650 nm. Zeta-potential and conductivity in solution were taken with the Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern).

Statistical analysis of fluorescence intensity data was performed in Microsoft Excel (version 2012). Statistical significance values for the means were assessed using one-way ANOVA (analysis of variance F-test). Differences were accepted as significant when the p-value was <0.05. After statistical evaluation, samples CD-MB synthesized with 8.54 % (CD-MBK06) and 10 % (CD-MBK07) of KMnO4 were selected for their performance to continue the analysis. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was obtained using the Shimadzu equipment of the SPM-9500J3 model. FT-IR spectra were collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Cr (VI) and Cr (III)
ce:section.ce:para: Samples CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07 were used to perform the fluorescence detection test of Cr (III) and Cr (VI) ions (prepared from Cr (III) salts and K2Cr2O7) dissolution, respectively). According to the National Environmental Council of Brazil, the allowed limit for Cr(VI) for treated wastewater discharge is 0.1 mg.L−1 and 1.0 mg.L−1 for Cr(III) [9]. Therefore, the selected analysis range was 0.1 mg.L−1 – 1.0 mg.L−1 for Cr (III) and 0.01 mg.L−1 – 1.0 mg.L−1 for Cr (VI) ions. Samples of CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07 were added to metal ion concentrations in a 3:1 ratio. The mixture was homogenized, and after 10 minutes, the luminescent emission spectra were obtained in triplicate. The corresponding fluorescence intensities of the solutions with Cr(III) and Cr(VI) with carbon dots were recorded (F). The fluorescence quenching was analyzed through the values of F/F0 , where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the samples of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of microalgae biomass and biochar
ce:section.ce:para: The main products of pyrolysis are a liquid fraction (bio-oil), solid (biochar), and gas. The main fraction generated in the pyrolysis of dry microalgae biomass was bio-oil, with 49.02 wt.% yield from biomass, followed by biochar with 31.23 wt.% yield from biomass, and finally 19.75 wt.% of non-condensable gas.

By analyzing the TGA/DTG of the dry biomass of the microalgae Chlorella Sorokiniana, it was possible to evaluate the temperature suitable for the pyrolysis reaction. Fig. 2 (a) presents the result of the TGA of the dry biomass. The first heating stage (up to 250 °C) involves the evaporation of sample moisture and light volatiles; the second stage comprises the devolatilization of the sample, between 250–500 °C. The weight dropped rapidly in this window, and two DTG peaks were identified corresponding to the decomposition at 282 °C and 332 °C of carbohydrates and proteins, respectively [8,67]. The last stage (above 500 °C) refers to the degradation of compounds, such as non-volatile carbon into CO and other compounds. Thus, the temperature chosen to perform the pyrolysis was 500 °C, as it is the temperature that comprises most of the devolatilization of the sample [24]. Fig. 2(b) shows the TGA of the MB generated in the pyrolysis. It is possible to observe that the biochar was the most thermally stable up to approximately 500 °C with negligible decomposition events [10].

Fig. 3 presents the DB and MB surface functional groups identified through FTIR analysis. The peak observed at 3278 cm−1 in the DB spectrum indicates the elongation vibration of the amine and hydroxyl groups. The broad band in approximately 3700–3200 cm−1 was associated with the OH group (phenol, water, and alcohol). Other bands commonly found in microalgae biomass are 1532 cm−1 (representing CN stretching and absorption of bending in the N single bond plane due to the amide in proteins) and 1031 cm−1 (representing esters) [5]. The FTIR spectra of the biomass showed most of the signals associated with the presence of carbon bonds (1634, 1532, 1453, 1386, 1230 cm−1). Bonds associated with aromatic carbons were the peaks at 1634, 1532, and 1453 cm −1 [39].

After the pyrolysis process, the MB spectrum lost intensity and sharpness in the peaks, showing a modification of the biochar structure. The 1250 cm−1 band vanished, indicating that the protein concentration was reduced. At high temperatures, ester species can form from carboxylic acids and alcohols, observed in the 1091 cm−1. Moreover, the peak at 887 cm−1 can be attributed to the formed aromatic bond [5].

After the carbon dot synthesis process, the samples showed high water solubility and a yellowish or dark brown color, commonly found in samples of carbon quantum dots (supplementary material, Fig. S2) [18]. The process of obtaining the CDs from the biochar was carried out with the solvent KMnO4 at different concentrations (0–10 %), setting the heating temperature to 121 °C and the reaction time (3 h). The yield of the CDs varied in each sample, emphasizing that the solvent concentration influenced the process. The highest yield (CD(g)/biochar(g)) was obtained by CD-MBK06 (3.30 %). Table 2 presents the results of sample yields and zeta-potential analysis. The Zeta-potential measured the degree of electrostatic repulsion between charged particles. The results show that the samples depolymerized with KMnO4 showed stability (negative charge). A higher negative charge results in repulsive force between molecules. This characteristic of (negatively charged) molecules facilitates interactions with other positively charged particles, such as metal ions [38].

To evaluate fluorescence performance, depolymerization with KMnO4 was performed at different concentrations and optimized considering the fluorescence intensity (FI). Fig. 4 shows the excitation-dependent emission (ranging from 300 to 450 nm) of carbon dots prepared with KMnO4 at several concentrations (0, 1.46, 5.00, 5.00, 8.54, and 10.00 %). The emission wavelength varies with the excitation wavelength (y-axis). The analyzed samples could emit fluorescence in the excitation wavelengths, decreasing the FI until reaching 625 nm. The maximal IF of the samples increased as the concentration of KMnO4 increased (Fig. 4 A-F). The results reinforced that KMnO4 can perform greater depolymerization of microalgae biochar since these samples showed higher fluorescence intensities, a characteristic of carbon dots [45]. The maximum emission achieved at an excitation wavelength was found in samples depolymerized with 8.54, and 10.00 % KMnO4 (CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07, respectively).

Considering the maximum fluorescence intensity values, a statistical analysis was performed and presented in Fig. 4G. The samples from the first block (CD-MBK01, CD-MBK02, CD-MBK03, and CD-MBK04) showed a statistical difference (p-value < 0.05) when comparing the fluorescence intensities with the samples from the second block (CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07). However, samples MBK01, CD-MBK02, CD-MBK03, and CD-MBK04 showed the same FI and CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07. It is possible to notice that the samples (depolymerized with KMnO4) CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07 presented the highest fluorescence intensity values, higher stability, and yield. The samples CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07 were selected to perform the chromium quantification analysis because they presented better performance.

FTIR spectra were analyzed (Fig. 5 ) to qualitatively identify sample compositions of CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07 after depolymerization. Both samples, CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07, showed similar peaks with different intensities. The peaks 3171 and 3179 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH. The band at 1592 cm−1 indicates vibrations of aromatic nuclei. The intense band near 1450 cm−1 indicates that the phenyl group is bonded to structures of the material or even bonded to atoms with lone pairs, which can be observed at 1445 cm−1 for both. They also exhibit a broad peak around 1445 cm−1, assigned to the D band (sp3-hybridized), indicating that they have a graphite-like structure [65]. The peaks 880–766 − 669 cm−1 and 880–765 − 666 cm−1 (CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07, respectively), can be attributed to vibrations of the aromatic carbons [44,51].

Fig. 6 shows the morphology of CDs through AFM microscopy. The CD-MBK06 showed an average particle height of 16.7 nm and a maximum of 43.5 nm. CD-MBK07 presented a maximum height of 115.2 mm and an average of 55.8 nm. [16] did thermal upcycling of the urine and synthesized CDs between 10–30 nm with a bimodal size distribution centered around 17 nm, and could reach 55 nm. While for urine (diet rich in asparagus) the size reached up to 80 nm. [54] presented similar results by analyzing the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of polymeric structures with diameters in the range of 100–200 nm when the synthesis was performed by pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine at 150° C. The author reported that as the temperature increased, the nanoparticles presented smaller sizes (for 200 °C, sizes between 10 and 15 nm, and between 250 °C and 300 °C presented particles around 5 nm).

In addition to the material used for the synthesis, the purification method is another factor that can influence the size of the carbon dots. Purification through dialysis tubes with different molecular weights can give different results. [64] found different sizes of carbon dots <5 nm, 10–20 nm, and 16–20 nm, when using dialysis tubes of 1000–3500 Da, 3500–7000 Da, and >7000 Da, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr (VI) and Cr (III) by fluorescence Spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The application of carbon dots as sensors of metallic metal ions (Cr (VI) and Cr (III)) was studied. A previous analysis of the fluorescence spectrum of Cr (VI) and Cr (III) was performed at a concentration of 1 mg.L−1 (Fig. S5) to choose the ideal excitation length for the quenching assay. The fluorescence spectrum made it possible to notice that the Cr (VI) emission showed peaks before 450 nm. At 470 nm, where the emission peaks of CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07 were centered, the intensity for Cr (VI) is close to zero, and the same is observed for Cr (III). Consequently, the fluorescence of Cr (VI) and Cr (III) does not influence the analysis performed at the emission wavelength of 470 nm. Therefore, the analyses were carried out along the emission wavelengths (400 nm to 625 nm), which were fixed at the excitation of 330 nm (maximum intensity).

Through Fig. 6E and Fig. 6F (CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07, respectively), it was possible to notice that the emission peaks are centered at 470 nm, for both. The ideal excitation wavelengths are 330 nm (Fig. S3 and Fig. S4) for both. The fluorescence spectra of CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07 with growing concentrations of Cr (VI) (0.01 mg.L−1, 0.1 mg.L−1, 1 mg.L−1, 10 mg.L−1, 50 mg.L−1 and 100 mg.L−1) and Cr (III) (0.1 mg.L−1, 1 mg.L−1, 10 mg.L−1, 50 mg.L−1 and 100 mg.L−1) are shown in Fig. 7 . The carbon dots were tested with application values for detection for Cr (VI) of 0.01 mg.L−1 and for Cr (III) of 0.1 mg.L−1, which are values below those allowed by [9].

The fluorescence intensity (FI) decreases when the Cr (VI) concentration increases for both (CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07). There is a linear relationship between the Cr (VI) concentration and the FI of CDs in 0.01–50 mg.L−1 (Fig. 8 ). The determination coefficient (R2) is 0.9993 and 0.9917 for CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07, respectively. The detection limit of our fluorescent probe is below the maximum Cr (VI) limit accepted by [9] (0.01 mg.L−1 in drinking water). The results showed that the sensor provided a promising method for detecting Cr (VI) with a wide detection range and low detection limit under ideal operating conditions.

For samples in which Cr (III) was added, the determination coefficient was 0.9975 and 0.9981 for CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07 in the range of 0.1–100 mg.L−1, respectively. These sensors can also detect Cr (III) from the maximum values allowed by [9] (0.1 mg.L−1 in drinking water) to values found in wastewater from tanneries of the leather industry, for example (40.53 ± 0.51 mg.L−1) [42].

Comparing the fluorescence profiles of the nanosensors (CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07) at a concentration of 100 mg.L−1, superior extinction was observed with the CD-MBK06 nanosensor. The CD-MBK06 sample showed affinity for Cr (VI) and Cr (III) with an accentuated extinction of 98 % and 90 %, respectively (Fig. 9 ). The CD-MBK07 nanosensor exhibited extinction, but with lower extinction for Cr (VI) and Cr (III) of 60 % and 46 %, respectively. Both nanosensors showed more selectivity for Cr (VI) when compared to Cr (III). Os resultados. Further additional tests should be carried out to know the influence of Cr (VI) and Cr (III) in the presence of other metals. In addition to a study that enables the use of nanosensors in real wastewater.

Different results were found when performing the above analysis at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm with the sample CD-MBK07. Through varying the different concentrations of Cr (III) or Cr (VI) it was possible to notice that there was an accentuated extinction of fluorescence in the presence of Cr (VI) which did not occur with Cr (III) (Fig. 10 ).

The carbon dots chemical, electronic and vibrational properties cause different dynamics and tempering levels to occur for the analyzed metals. Efficient fluorescence quenching indicates interaction between heavy metal ions and fluorophores. This effect has been attributed to energy transfer, an electron transfer mechanism, or a combination of both. Cr (III) and Cr (VI) ions can accept or donate electrons to CDots to form metallic complexes [4]. This is favored by the bonds presented (C single bond O, C double bond O and C single bond OH) that facilitate bonds with the analyzed metals. In addition, they may have phosphorus or nitrogen groups since the microalgae biomass is concentrated in these compounds that facilitate the binding of metals with CDs [44,56].

The extinction of 84.9 ± 0.03 % for Cr (VI) against 51.2 ± 0.02 % for Cr (III) shows that the CD-MBK07 nanosensor has a selectivity for Cr (VI) at this wavelength. This phenomenon was not observed for the sample CD-MBK06 at the wavelength of 350 nm. The complexity of the selectivity presented by the CD-MBK07 sample at 350 nm wavelength needs to be studied in the future. [43] reported the synthesis of carbon dots from microalgae for the first time. The metals studied by the authors were Pb2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, and Ni2+. Thus, this is the first study that uses carbon dots from microalgae biochar for Cr (VI) and Cr (III) detection. Similar results were found for chrome using carbon dot decorated natural kyanite nanoparticles (Bardhan et al., 2022).

The coefficient of determination and linear relationship between the Cr (VI) or Cr (III) concentration and the FI are shown in Table 3 . The adjustment parameters obtained from the Cr (VI) and Cr (III) metal ion quenching experiments at 350 nm quench length for the CD-MBK07 nanosensor are described. The detection and analysis of the toxic ionic species of Cr (VI) and Cr (III) in wastewater is a crucial area of research. Various chemical reagents and the different methods that require expensive instrumentation used in the past for detecting and determining non-biodegradable Cr (VI) and Cr(III) ions present severe limitations. [12]. The results show that the proposed sensing system has high accuracy and reproducibility.

In this paper a method to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) from a pyrolysis biorrefinery co-product (microalgae biochar) by a hydrothermal method with KMnO4 was demonstrated. Samples containing carbon dots showed fluorescence intensity results in addition to stability. CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07 (biochar polymerized with 8.54 % and 10.0 % KMnO4, respectively) were selected to follow extinction tests with Chromium (VI) and Chromium (III) because they presented stability and the highest fluorescence intensity. CD-MBK06 and CD-MBK07 fluorescence was suppressed by Chromium (VI) and Chromium (III). The correlation between fluorescence reduction and metal ion concentration was established for the metal ions (Cr (III) and Cr (VI)), including the detection range (0.1 mg.L−1–100 mg.L−1, Cr (III) and 0.01 mg.L−1–100 mg.L−1, Cr (VI)). The CD-MBK07 sample (biochar polymerized with 10.0 % KMnO4) showed higher selectivity for Chromium (VI) when analyzed at the extinction length of 350 nm. The CDs were synthesized in a renewable way and can be used as sensors for the metal Chromium (VI) and Chromium (III). The advantage of using residual biochar biorefineries presents the possibility of coupling the production of nanomaterials with the biorefinery concept. The development of new co-products, as demonstrated herein, is critical for biorefineries to achieve economic feasibility.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the Agency Financier of Studies and Projects (FINEP) for the financial support (MCTI/FINEP CT-HIDRO 01/2013), the Research Support Foundation of the State of Rio Grande do Sul (Fapergs) and resources and the scholarships financed by Coordination for the Improvement of Higher Education Personnel (CAPES).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2022.09.047.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
